title: Response Time SLA Guide
summary: Understand current latency, target latency, SLA threshold, and error budget before tuning the rest of the performance stack.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool compares current and target response times against an SLA threshold so downstream performance planning starts from a defined latency boundary.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch performance pressure before it becomes production trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when defining service-level expectations, validating response-time targets, or documenting how much latency risk the system can tolerate before tuning throughput, queues, concurrency, and resource utilization.
bullets: Early performance planning.
Expansion or load-growth review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining performance tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical performance planning terms.

items.label: Current Avg Latency (ms)
items.description: The measured or expected average response time at the current operating point.
items.whyItMatters: This is the baseline the rest of the performance lane inherits. If the current value is already near the SLA, later scaling assumptions have very little room to work.
items.commonMistake: Using a best-case test value instead of a normal production average.

links.label: Compare latency and throughput pressure
links.note: Use this when the SLA question depends on rising throughput.

items.label: Target Avg Latency (ms)
items.description: The response time you want the system to stay under after tuning or scaling.
items.whyItMatters: This becomes the planning target for queueing, concurrency, CPU, disk, network, and cache decisions.
items.commonMistake: Setting a target that is technically possible in testing but unrealistic under peak load.

links.label: See throughput tradeoffs
links.note: Latency targets often change once load increases.

items.label: SLA Threshold (ms)
items.description: The maximum average response time allowed by the SLA or internal service objective.
items.whyItMatters: This defines the hard boundary for the analysis. The closer current or target latency sits to this line, the less operating margin remains.
items.commonMistake: Treating the SLA threshold as the normal operating target instead of a limit.

items.label: Allowed Error Budget (%)
items.description: The acceptable amount of service miss, latency violation, or instability before the SLA is considered unhealthy.
items.whyItMatters: A small error budget makes the design less tolerant of bursts, maintenance, retries, or transient overload.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring error budget and only comparing one average latency number.

links.label: Relate error budget to headroom
links.note: Error budget and headroom both describe how much margin is left.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the latency target has useful reserve under the SLA threshold. Watch means the system is close enough to the boundary that small regressions could matter. Risk means the current or target latency is likely too close to, or over, the SLA boundary.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the architecture, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most performance planning mistakes come from looking at one symptom in isolation instead of tracing how latency, throughput, queues, concurrency, and resource pressure interact.
bullets: Sizing for average behavior while ignoring peak periods, bursts, retries, and background work.
Tuning one layer without checking whether the bottleneck simply moves to another resource.
Treating theoretical maximums as safe sustained production capacity.
Failing to document the assumptions behind latency targets, utilization limits, and growth margin.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace application testing, manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Measurements from the real platform are better than guesses whenever they are available.
Application behavior, storage design, network path, cache behavior, and workload mix can change the result.
Healthy results still need monitoring and validation after deployment.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, explain, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, production testing, vendor validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, software, platform, and network path being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, operating requirements, and SLA language.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or workload behavior changes.
